message:
test(types): add Track R record encoding unit tests (bd-gieaf)

Add comprehensive unit tests for record encoding in fsqlite-types:

- bd_gieaf_record_scratch_reuse_1000_records_no_realloc_after_warmup:
  Verifies Vec scratch buffer achieves zero reallocations after warmup
  (100 rows), demonstrating stable capacity for 900 subsequent inserts

- bd_gieaf_record_max_columns_128_roundtrip:
  Tests encode/decode of 128-column records with mixed types (NULL,
  INTEGER, FLOAT, TEXT, BLOB)

- bd_gieaf_record_all_types_comprehensive_coverage:
  Covers all SqliteValue variants including edge cases (0, 1, -1,
  boundary integers, special floats, empty/large text/blob)

- bd_gieaf_prop_scratch_buffer_matches_fresh_allocation:
  Proptest (10K cases) verifying serialize_record_iter_into produces
  identical output to serialize_record

Also updates bead status for bd-gieaf (closed), bd-obixt (closed with
audit note), and bd-abgqx (closed).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

W-TEST must validate the classifier, not just generic "direct vs fallback" behavior.

### Required truth matrix
For each prepared INSERT shape, assert the selected lane and the fallback reason if not direct:
- pure parameter values
- parameter + literals
- cheap arithmetic expressions
- cheap concat expressions
- function-call expressions
- AUTOINCREMENT / explicit IPK variants
- indexed vs non-indexed tables
- RETURNING / trigger / FK guarded shapes

### Required runtime logging
Emit and assert:
- lane chosen at prepare time
- sticky fallback transitions
- direct-lane hit counts
- reusable-lane hit counts
- ambiguous-shape sampling decisions if adaptive classification is used
","created_at":"2026-04-06T16:07:36Z"},{"id":3495,"issue_id":"bd-aaiwu","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Revision: deterministic e2e manifest required

W-TEST is not complete with unit tests alone.

### Required deliverables
- deterministic e2e script manifest with scenario ids
- canonical invocation contract for each script
- required environment and artifact paths
- structured log schema with trace ids, timings, counters, and fallback reasons
- before/after benchmark artifact bundle for the gated cells

### Minimum scenario families
- autocommit pure write
- same-connection mixed read/write overlap
- bulk single-txn insert
- prepared UPDATE/DELETE loop
- concurrent-writer non-regression
","created_at":"2026-04-06T16:14:06Z"},{"id":3504,"issue_id":"bd-aaiwu","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Benchmark governance and evidence contract

Track W has already accumulated contradictory historical claims across focused benches, comprehensive benches, and transient reruns. W-TEST must make that impossible going forward.

### Every performance claim must record
- exact artifact path
- exact git commit / dirty-tree state
- benchmark command
- build profile
- host / CPU identity
- dataset scale and SQL shape / scenario id

### Closure rule
If a focused benchmark and the comprehensive benchmark disagree materially about a touched workload, the bead does **not** close until the discrepancy is explained and the authoritative source for that claim is documented.

### Required artifact bundle
- before/after benchmark artifacts for each gated cell
- structured log bundle with trace ids matching the scenario manifest
- short human-readable ledger summarizing which artifact is authoritative for each claimed win or non-regression
","created_at":"2026-04-06T18:46:16Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-afyec","title":"MVCC hot-path optimization tranche 1","description":"Round-1 MVCC performance/reliability tranche.

Checklist:
- [ ] remove avoidable alloc/sort work from FCW validation and commit publication paths
- [ ] cache or otherwise avoid O(active_txns) GC-horizon scans on core hot path
- [ ] stop paying heavy CommitIndex fallback-shard update cost for fast-array pages
- [ ] remove bogus/expensive EBR retirement bookkeeping in publish path if not reclaiming actual retired versions
- [ ] preserve correctness with updated tests for FCW/GC/EBR semantics
- [ ] run targeted rch-backed checks/tests/benches and record blockers if bench harness still has feature-path friction

Constraints:
- concurrent writer mode remains ON by default
- no behavior regressions in FCW/SSI semantics
- all cargo work via rch

Success criteria:
- hot-path code is measurably leaner or at minimum structurally cheaper
- tests updated to reflect true semantics rather than bookkeeping artifacts

Workspace gates remain blocked by unrelated existing issues in crates/fsqlite-e2e/src/perf_runner.rs (missing HotPathProfileSnapshot fields), /data/projects/asupersync/src/cx/cx.rs (upstream unused-variable warning under clippy), and crates/fsqlite-core/tests/conformance_oracle_s55.rs (existing clippy single_element_loop findings).","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-18T13:55:05.379687170Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-18T22:51:41.751640986Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2464,"issue_id":"bd-afyec","author":"FrostyBirch","text":"2026-03-18 FrostyBirch: continued MVCC hot-path tranche work in crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/begin_concurrent.rs on top of the existing dirty-tree exact-release patch. No change to concurrent_mode defaults or file-locking semantics.","created_at":"2026-03-18T17:38:27Z"},{"id":2465,"issue_id":"bd-afyec","author":"ubuntu","text":"2026-03-18 TealStream read-only pass on the exact-held-lock release cut in crates/fsqlite-mvcc/src/begin_concurrent.rs: FrostyBirch currently holds the file reservation, so I did not edit.  {"id":"bd-agozb","title":"Q1: FlatHashPageCache with modulo-indexed bucket array and chain pointers","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:30:45.710023595Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-01T20:31:53.835951879Z","closed_at":"2026-04-01T20:31:53.835624486Z","close_reason":"Deferred: parking_lot::Mutex uncontended is ~2-3ns. The if-let-Some branch check per access is the real overhead, not the lock. The FastPageArray from F1 already provides the key speedup (direct index vs hash). Further optimization is diminishing returns.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":3433,"issue_id":"bd-agozb","author":"ubuntu","text":"## What
Replace ShardedPageCache internals with a flat array hash table matching C SQLite's pcache1 pattern.

## Exact Implementation
File: crates/fsqlite-pager/src/page_cache.rs

1. New struct FlatHashPageCache:
   - buckets: Vec<Option<PageSlot>> where PageSlot = { pgno, data: PageBuf, next_chain: u32 }
   - n_buckets: usize (power of 2 for fast modulo via bitwise AND)
   - Lookup: bucket_idx = pgno.get() & (n_buckets - 1); walk chain
   
2. For single-connection: wrap in RefCell (zero-cost interior mutability)
   For multi-connection: wrap each bucket in AtomicPtr (CAS, not Mutex)

3. Keep ShardedPageCache as fallback for concurrent mode until Q2 is done

## C SQLite Reference
pcache1.c:1000-1068: pcache1FetchNoMutex()
- ppPage = &pCache->apHash[iKey % pCache->nHash] — single array index
- Walk pPage->pNext chain for collisions
- Mark page as pinned by removing from LRU

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Page lookup latency < 15 ns (from current 45-50 ns)
2. Single-connection :memory: INSERT benchmark improved ≥ 5%
3. conformance_oracle_s55 passes
4. No regression in concurrent writer throughput

## Risk & Fallback
Risk: Chain collisions could degrade to O(n). Mitigation: resize when load factor > 0.75.
Fallback: Feature flag `flat_hash_page_cache` defaulting true; false reverts to ShardedPageCache.
","created_at":"2026-04-01T18:30:46Z"},{"id":3449,"issue_id":"bd-agozb","author":"ubuntu","text":"## CORRECTION (Review): FastPageArray Already Exists (from F1 commit 9abedc20)

The flat page array (FastPageArray at page_cache.rs:398) was already implemented in commit 9abedc20. However, it's wrapped in `Mutex<FastPageArray>` (line 720), which means EVERY access still acquires a mutex lock (~5ns uncontended).

### Revised Q1 Scope
Q1 should NOT create a new data structure. Instead, it should:
1. Replace `Mutex<FastPageArray>` with `UnsafeCell<FastPageArray>` for single-connection mode (zero locking — SAFE because only one thread accesses)
2. OR use `RefCell<FastPageArray>` (runtime borrow check, ~1ns vs Mutex's ~5ns)
3. Gate on single_connection_count == 1 (already tracked via Arc<AtomicUsize>)

### Why RefCell is Safe Here
Single-connection :memory: mode means exactly one thread accesses the cache. RefCell's runtime borrow check catches any accidental concurrent access in debug builds. In release builds, the borrow check is ~1ns (vs Mutex ~5ns).

### Alternative: Just Remove Mutex for Single-Conn
If we can prove (via the connection_count atomic) that only one connection exists, we can use UnsafeCell with a safety comment. This is what C SQLite does — it simply doesn't lock in single-thread mode.

 {"id":"bd-gdpez","title":"WASM T.5: Automated binary size tracking and regression alerts","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:28:02.124243443Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-02T06:40:33.044012758Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-gdpez","depends_on_id":"bd-3inia","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1759,"issue_id":"bd-gdpez","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# T.5 Detailed Implementation Spec: Binary Size Tracking and Regression Alerts

## Goal
Track WASM binary size over time, detect regressions per-PR, and maintain a historical dashboard. Keeps the team accountable for binary bloat — every extra KB matters for initial page load.

## Components

### 1. Size Recording Script
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# scripts/record-wasm-size.sh
# Run after wasm-pack build to record size metrics

WASM_FILE="${1:-packages/core/wasm/frankensqlite_bg.wasm}"
METRICS_FILE="metrics/wasm-size.json"

if [ ! -f "$WASM_FILE" ]; then
  echo "ERROR: WASM file not found: $WASM_FILE"
  exit 1
fi

RAW_SIZE=$(stat -c%s "$WASM_FILE")
GZ_SIZE=$(gzip -c "$WASM_FILE" | wc -c)
BR_SIZE=$(brotli -c "$WASM_FILE" | wc -c)
COMMIT=$(git rev-parse HEAD)
DATE=$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

echo "[size-track] Raw: $(numfmt --to=iec $RAW_SIZE)"
echo "[size-track] Gzip: $(numfmt --to=iec $GZ_SIZE)"
echo "[size-track] Brotli: $(numfmt --to=iec $BR_SIZE)"
echo "[size-track] Commit: $COMMIT"

# Append to metrics JSONL (one record per line)
echo "{\"date\":\"$DATE\",\"commit\":\"$COMMIT\",\"raw\":$RAW_SIZE,\"gzip\":$GZ_SIZE,\"brotli\":$BR_SIZE}" >> metrics/wasm-size.jsonl

echo "[size-track] Recorded to $METRICS_FILE"
```

### 2. PR Comment Bot (GitHub Actions)
```yaml
# Part of wasm-ci.yml, in the size-check job:
- name: Comment PR with size
  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
  uses: actions/github-script@v7
  with:
    script: |
      const fs = require('fs');
      const { execSync } = require('child_process');

      const wasmFile = 'packages/core/wasm/frankensqlite_bg.wasm';
      const rawSize = fs.statSync(wasmFile).size;
      const gzSize = parseInt(execSync(`gzip -c ${wasmFile} | wc -c`).toString().trim());
      const brSize = parseInt(execSync(`brotli -c ${wasmFile} | wc -c`).toString().trim());

      // Get baseline from main branch
      let baseRaw = 0, baseGz = 0;
      try {
        const baseline = execSync('git show origin/main:metrics/wasm-size.jsonl 2>/dev/null || true')
          .toString().trim().split('\n').filter(Boolean).pop();
        if (baseline) {
          const base = JSON.parse(baseline);
          baseRaw = base.raw;
          baseGz = base.gzip;
        }
      } catch {}

      const fmtSize = (n) => {
        if (n >= 1024*1024) return (n/(1024*1024)).toFixed(1) + ' MiB';
        return (n/1024).toFixed(1) + ' KiB';
      };
      const fmtDelta = (curr, base) => {
        if (!base) return 'N/A (no baseline)';
        const delta = curr - base;
        const pct = ((delta / base) * 100).toFixed(1);
        const sign = delta >= 0 ? '+' : '';
        const emoji = delta > 1024 ? '🔴' : delta > 0 ? '🟡' : '🟢';
        return `${emoji} ${sign}${fmtSize(delta)} (${sign}${pct}%)`;
      };

      const body = `## WASM Binary Size Report

| Metric | Size | Delta vs main |
|--------|------|---------------|
| Raw | ${fmtSize(rawSize)} | ${fmtDelta(rawSize, baseRaw)} |
| Gzipped | ${fmtSize(gzSize)} | ${fmtDelta(gzSize, baseGz)} |
| Brotli | ${fmtSize(brSize)} | N/A |

<details><summary>Size Budget</summary>

| Budget | Limit | Status |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Gzipped | 2 MiB | ${gzSize > 2*1024*1024 ? '❌ OVER' : '✅ OK'} |
| Raw | 8 MiB | ${rawSize > 8*1024*1024 ? '❌ OVER' : '✅ OK'} |

</details>`;

      // Find existing comment or create new
      const { data: comments } = await github.rest.issues.listComments({
        owner: context.repo.owner,
        repo: context.repo.repo,
        issue_number: context.issue.number,
      });
      const existing = comments.find(c => c.body.includes('WASM Binary Size Report'));

      if (existing) {
        await github.rest.issues.updateComment({
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          comment_id: existing.id,
          body,
        });
      } else {
        await github.rest.issues.createComment({
          owner: context.repo.owner,
          repo: context.repo.repo,
          issue_number: context.issue.number,
          body,
        });
      }

### 3. Size Budget Configuration
```json
// metrics/size-budget.json
{
  "limits": {
    "raw_max_bytes": 8388608,
    "gzip_max_bytes": 2097152,
    "gzip_warn_bytes": 1572864
  },
  "tracking": {
    "file": "metrics/wasm-size.jsonl",
    "format": "jsonl"
  }
}
```

### 4. Local Size Check (Pre-push hook)
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# scripts/check-wasm-size.sh — run locally before push
WASM_FILE="${1:-packages/core/wasm/frankensqlite_bg.wasm}"
BUDGET_FILE="metrics/size-budget.json"

if [ ! -f "$WASM_FILE" ]; then
  echo "[size-check] No WASM build found, skipping"
  exit 0
fi

GZ_SIZE=$(gzip -c "$WASM_FILE" | wc -c)
MAX=$(python3 -c "import json; print(json.load(open('$BUDGET_FILE'))['limits']['gzip_max_bytes'])")

echo "[size-check] Gzipped: $(numfmt --to=iec $GZ_SIZE) / $(numfmt --to=iec $MAX)"
if [ "$GZ_SIZE" -gt "$MAX" ]; then
  echo "[size-check] ERROR: WASM binary exceeds gzip budget!"
  echo "[size-check] Run 'cargo bloat --target wasm32-unknown-unknown' to identify large functions"
  exit 1
fi
echo "[size-check] OK"
```

## Bloat Investigation Commands
When size regresses, developers can run:
```bash
# Top 20 largest functions in WASM binary
cargo bloat --target wasm32-unknown-unknown --release -n 20

# Compare two builds
twiggy diff old.wasm new.wasm

# Tree map of WASM sections
twiggy top new.wasm -n 30
```

## Acceptance Criteria
1. Every CI run records raw/gzip/brotli sizes to metrics JSONL
2. PR comments show size delta vs main branch with visual indicators
3. Size budget of 2 MiB gzipped enforced as CI gate
4. Warning threshold at 1.5 MiB gzipped (yellow indicator)
5. Historical data preserved in repo for trend tracking
6. `cargo bloat` / `twiggy` commands documented for investigation
7. Local pre-push check script available for developers

 {"id":"bd-ggxs","title":"§9 Cx Parameter Enforcement + Sealed Trait Pattern Validation","description":"## SUMMARY
Cross-cutting enforcement bead for §9 (Trait Hierarchy):

1. **Cx Everywhere Rule:** any trait method that touches I/O, acquires locks, or could block MUST accept `&Cx` as its first parameter.
2. **Sealed Trait Pattern:** internal-only traits MUST be sealed to prevent downstream implementations that could violate MVCC safety invariants; user-extension traits MUST remain implementable.

This bead exists to make these rules mechanically checkable (tests + audits) so they cannot regress silently.

## Scope

### Cx Everywhere
- MUST take `&Cx`: VFS I/O, pager operations, cursor operations, checkpoint/writer I/O, coordinator IPC, and any other potentially-blocking path.
- MUST NOT take `&Cx`: pure computation only (example: `CollationFunction::compare`; CPU-only `ScalarFunction::call`).

### Sealed Traits
Open extension points (user-implementable; MUST NOT be sealed):
- `Vfs`, `VfsFile`
- `ScalarFunction`, `AggregateFunction`, `WindowFunction`
- `VirtualTable`, `VirtualTableCursor`
- `CollationFunction`, `Authorizer`

Internal-only (MUST be sealed):
- `MvccPager`
- `BtreeCursorOps`
- `CheckpointPageWriter`

### Test Mocks (Required)
The defining crate for each sealed trait MUST export test mocks usable cross-crate:
- `MockMvccPager`
- `MockBtreeCursor` (implements `BtreeCursorOps`)
- `MockCheckpointPageWriter`

## Unit Test Requirements

- `test_cx_param_audit_vfs_traits`: every I/O method on `Vfs`/`VfsFile` takes `&Cx`.
- `test_cx_param_audit_mvcc_pager_trait`: `begin/get_page/write_page/allocate_page/free_page/commit/rollback` all take `&Cx`.
- `test_cx_param_audit_btree_cursor_ops_trait`: all cursor ops take `&Cx`.
- `test_cx_param_audit_checkpoint_page_writer_trait`: `write_page/sync` take `&Cx`.
- `test_pure_compute_exclusion_collation_compare_no_cx`: `CollationFunction::compare` does not take `&Cx`.
- `test_pure_compute_exclusion_scalar_call_cpu_only_no_cx`: CPU-only `ScalarFunction::call` does not take `&Cx`.
- `test_sealed_mvcc_pager_compile_fail`: downstream impl attempt fails due to sealed trait.
- `test_sealed_btree_cursor_ops_compile_fail`: downstream impl attempt fails.
- `test_sealed_checkpoint_page_writer_compile_fail`: downstream impl attempt fails.
- `test_open_vfs_external_impl_compiles`: downstream crate/module can implement `Vfs`/`VfsFile` (compile-pass).
- `test_open_function_traits_external_impl_compiles`: downstream can implement `ScalarFunction`/`AggregateFunction`/`WindowFunction` (compile-pass).
- `test_mock_exports_available`: mocks are exported and usable from other crates' tests.

## E2E Test

- `test_e2e_bd_ggxs_cx_and_sealed_enforcement_workspace_pass`:
  - Run the signature audit over the whole workspace.
  - Run compile-fail tests for sealed traits and compile-pass tests for open traits.
  - Run a small cancellation smoke (deadline-expired `Cx` causes a real I/O path to fail fast).
  - Emit a deterministic JSON report (see Logging Requirements).

## Logging Requirements

- INFO: enforcement summary: `methods_checked`, `violations`, `compile_fail_cases`, `compile_pass_cases`.
- ERROR: each violation with `crate`, `trait`, `method`, and the observed signature.
- Artifact: deterministic JSON report, e.g. `target/cx_sealed_enforcement_report.json`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All I/O / lock-acquiring / blocking trait methods accept `&Cx` as first parameter.
- [ ] Pure computation methods explicitly excluded from `Cx` (do not take it).
- [ ] `MvccPager`, `BtreeCursorOps`, and `CheckpointPageWriter` are sealed; downstream impl attempts fail.
- [ ] User-extensible traits are NOT sealed; downstream impl attempts compile.
- [ ] Mocks for sealed traits exist and are usable cross-crate.
- [ ] E2E enforcement pass produces a deterministic JSON report and fails CI on any violation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:42:55.654161787Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T20:32:34.686395347Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T20:32:34.686369178Z","close_reason":"Implemented Cx+sealed enforcement suite, cross-crate mocks, open trait exports, trybuild compile-fail/pass checks, and deterministic report artifact; validation tests passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ggxs","depends_on_id":"bd-3go.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-ggxs","depends_on_id":"bd-8kd","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1760,"issue_id":"bd-ggxs","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# §9 Cx Parameter Enforcement + Sealed Trait Pattern Validation

## Spec Reference
COMPREHENSIVE_SPEC_FOR_FRANKENSQLITE_V1.md lines 12422-12455 (§9)

## Scope

Ensure the "Cx Everywhere" rule is enforced across all I/O and lock-acquiring
trait methods, and that internal-only traits use the sealed trait pattern to
prevent downstream implementations that could violate MVCC safety invariants.

## Cx Everywhere Rule (from spec)

Every trait method that touches I/O, acquires locks, or could block MUST accept
`&Cx` as its first parameter. This enables:
- **Cancellation:** Any operation can be cancelled by the caller's context
- **Deadline propagation:** Timeout budgets flow through the entire call chain
- **Capability narrowing:** Callers can restrict what callees are allowed to do

Pure computation (e.g., `CollationFunction::compare`, `ScalarFunction::call` for
CPU-only work) does NOT take `Cx`. When in doubt, include `Cx`.

## Sealed Trait Pattern (from spec)

```rust
mod sealed { pub trait Sealed {} }  // private to the defining crate

pub trait MvccPager: sealed::Sealed + Send + Sync { /* ... */ }
```

**Open extension points (user-implementable):** `Vfs`, `VfsFile`,
`ScalarFunction`, `AggregateFunction`, `WindowFunction`, `VirtualTable`,
`VirtualTableCursor`, `CollationFunction`, `Authorizer`.

**Internal-only (sealed):** `MvccPager`, `BtreeCursorOps` (and any similar
trait whose implementations must preserve engine invariants).

Test mocks for sealed traits live alongside the trait definition and are
exported as values/types for other crates to use in tests.

## Unit Test Specifications

### Test 1: Cx parameter audit — VFS traits
Programmatically (or via compile-time assertion) verify that every method on
the `Vfs` and `VfsFile` traits that performs I/O takes `&Cx` as the first
parameter. Methods: `open`, `delete`, `access`, `full_pathname`, `read`,
`write`, `truncate`, `sync`, `file_size`, `lock`, `unlock`, `check_reserved_lock`.

### Test 2: Cx parameter audit — MvccPager trait
Verify that all MvccPager methods that acquire locks or perform I/O take `&Cx`:
`begin`, `acquire_page`, `release_page`, `commit`, `rollback`, `checkpoint`.

### Test 3: Pure computation exclusion
Verify that `CollationFunction::compare` and `ScalarFunction::call` (when
CPU-only) do NOT take `&Cx`. This confirms the rule is not over-applied.

### Test 4: Sealed trait — MvccPager cannot be implemented externally
Write a compile-fail test (using `trybuild` or `compile_fail` doctest):
```rust
struct FakePager;
impl MvccPager for FakePager { ... }  // MUST fail: Sealed not implemented
```
Verify the compiler error mentions the sealed trait bound.

### Test 5: Sealed trait — BtreeCursorOps cannot be implemented externally
Same pattern as Test 4 for `BtreeCursorOps`. Verify compile-fail.

### Test 6: Open traits CAN be implemented externally
Write a positive test: implement `Vfs` and `VfsFile` in a downstream test
crate/module. Verify it compiles and can be used. This confirms sealed is
NOT applied to user-extensible traits.

### Test 7: Test mock for sealed trait is available
Verify that the crate defining `MvccPager` exports a `MockMvccPager` (or
similar) that implements the sealed trait. Verify it can be used in tests
from other crates.

### Test 8: Cancellation via Cx works end-to-end
Create a `Cx` with a deadline in the past. Call a VFS `read` method with it.
Verify the operation returns a cancellation/timeout error rather than
performing I/O.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All I/O and lock-acquiring trait methods confirmed to take `&Cx`
- Pure computation methods confirmed to NOT take `&Cx`
- `MvccPager` and `BtreeCursorOps` confirmed sealed via compile-fail tests
- User-extensible traits (Vfs, VfsFile, etc.) confirmed NOT sealed
- Test mocks for sealed traits are available for cross-crate testing
- Cx cancellation propagation works end-to-end
- All tests pass under `cargo test`

 {"id":"bd-gird","title":"§10.7-10.8 VDBE Instruction Format + Coroutines","description":"## SUMMARY

Defines the VDBE (Virtual Database Engine) instruction format and the coroutine mechanism used for subqueries and CTEs. The VdbeOp struct is a fixed-size 6-field instruction with opcode (u8), three i32 operands (p1/p2/p3), a polymorphic P4 extended operand, and a u16 flags field (p5). The coroutine subsystem uses InitCoroutine/Yield/EndCoroutine opcodes to implement cooperative PC-swapping between outer queries and CTE/subquery bodies without materializing entire result sets.

## KEY DATA STRUCTURES / ALGORITHMS

- **VdbeOp struct**: `{ opcode: Opcode (u8), p1: i32, p2: i32, p3: i32, p4: P4, p5: u16 }`. 190+ opcodes covering control flow, data access, comparison, arithmetic, string, row operations, cursor, sorting, aggregation, transactions.
- **P4 enum**: `None | Int32(i32) | Int64(i64) | Real(f64) | String(String) | Blob(Vec<u8>) | FuncDef(Arc<dyn ScalarFunction>) | CollSeq(Arc<dyn CollationFunction>) | KeyInfo(KeyInfo) | Mem(Mem) | Vtab(Arc<dyn VirtualTable>) | Table(TableInfo) | Subprogram(VdbeProgram)`.
- **Label system for jump resolution**: `emit_label()` returns a `Label` handle; `resolve_label(label, address)` patches all forward-referencing instructions. All labels MUST be resolved before execution begins.
- **Register allocation**: Registers numbered starting at 1. Sequential allocation via `alloc_reg()` / `alloc_regs(n)`. Temporary registers returned to pool after use; persistent registers held for statement lifetime.
- **Coroutine mechanism**: `InitCoroutine r_yield, P2, <cte_body>` sets r_yield to CTE body PC. `Yield r_yield` swaps PCs (saves current PC into r_yield, jumps to old r_yield). `EndCoroutine r_yield` performs final swap back to caller. NOT async -- cooperative state machines within the VDBE VM.

## NORMATIVE INVARIANTS

1. p5 is u16 matching C SQLite's declaration; per-opcode P5 flag usage MUST match C SQLite exactly.
2. All labels MUST be resolved before execution begins; unresolved labels are a codegen bug.
3. Register numbering starts at 1 (not 0).
4. Coroutines are NOT async; they are cooperative PC-swap state machines.
5. `Yield` saves current PC into r_yield and jumps to old r_yield (bidirectional swap).
6. `EndCoroutine` marks exhaustion and performs final return to caller.
7. In `BEGIN CONCURRENT`, `OP_NewRowid` MUST allocate via the snapshot-independent RowId allocator (per section 5.10.1.1), NOT by scanning snapshot-visible max(rowid).

## UNIT TEST REQUIREMENTS

1. `test_vdbe_op_struct_size` -- VdbeOp struct fields are accessible and correctly typed (opcode u8, p1/p2/p3 i32, p4 P4 enum, p5 u16).
2. `test_p4_variant_all_types` -- Each P4 variant (None, Int32, Int64, Real, String, Blob, FuncDef, CollSeq, KeyInfo, Mem, Vtab, Table, Subprogram) can be constructed and matched.
3. `test_label_emit_and_resolve` -- emit_label returns distinct handles; resolve_label patches instruction p2 targets; unresolved label detection panics/errors.
4. `test_register_alloc_sequential` -- alloc_reg returns 1, 2, 3, ...; alloc_regs(3) returns contiguous block.
5. `test_register_temp_pool_reuse` -- Temporary registers are returned to pool and reused in subsequent allocations.
6. `test_coroutine_init_yield_end` -- InitCoroutine sets r_yield correctly; Yield swaps PCs bidirectionally; EndCoroutine returns to caller and marks exhaustion.
7. `test_coroutine_multi_row_production` -- CTE body yields 5 rows; outer query consumes all 5 via Yield loop.
8. `test_all_opcode_dispatch_coverage` -- Every Opcode enum variant has a matching handler in the dispatch table (no unimplemented arms).
9. `test_p5_flags_u16_range` -- p5 values above 0xFF are accepted (confirms u16, not u8).

## E2E TEST

Execute `WITH cte AS (SELECT 1 UNION ALL SELECT x+1 FROM cte WHERE x < 5) SELECT * FROM cte` through the full pipeline (parse -> plan -> codegen -> VDBE execution). Verify the coroutine mechanism produces rows [1,2,3,4,5] without materializing the entire CTE. Compare result against C SQLite output.

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA

- VdbeOp struct matches the 6-field layout specified in section 10.7 exactly.
- All P4 variants are implemented and functional.
- Label system correctly resolves forward jumps; unresolved labels produce errors before execution.
- Register allocator produces sequential numbering starting at 1, with temp pool reuse.
- Coroutine InitCoroutine/Yield/EndCoroutine cycle produces correct row-by-row iteration for CTEs and subqueries.
- All 190+ opcodes have dispatch handlers (no panics on valid opcode).
- WITH RECURSIVE queries execute correctly using coroutine mechanism.","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-08T06:03:25.877561327Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T21:28:09.206953788Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T21:28:09.206924563Z","close_reason":"Implemented VDBE program builder, label system, register allocator, coroutine mechanism, KeyInfo, disassembler in fsqlite-vdbe. ### Spec Content (Lines 13578-13650)

**VDBE Instruction Format:**
Each instruction is a fixed-size struct:
```
VdbeOp := {
  opcode  : u8,          // operation code (OP_Init, OP_Goto, OP_Column, etc.)
  p1      : i32,         // first operand (register number, cursor number, etc.)
  p2      : i32,         // second operand (jump target, count, etc.)
  p3      : i32,         // third operand
  p4      : P4Union,     // polymorphic fourth operand (string, function ptr, etc.)
  p5      : u16,         // flags/modifiers
}
```

**Opcode categories (all from C SQLite, must be implemented):**
- Control flow: OP_Init, OP_Goto, OP_If, OP_IfNot, OP_Halt, OP_Return
- Data access: OP_OpenRead, OP_OpenWrite, OP_Column, OP_Rowid
- Comparison: OP_Eq, OP_Ne, OP_Lt, OP_Le, OP_Gt, OP_Ge
- Arithmetic: OP_Add, OP_Subtract, OP_Multiply, OP_Divide, OP_Remainder
- String: OP_Concat, OP_Function
- Row operations: OP_Insert, OP_Delete, OP_NewRowid, OP_MakeRecord
- Cursor: OP_Rewind, OP_Next, OP_Prev, OP_SeekGE, OP_SeekGT, OP_SeekLE, OP_SeekLT
- Sorting: OP_SorterOpen, OP_SorterInsert, OP_SorterSort, OP_SorterNext
- Aggregation: OP_AggStep, OP_AggFinal
- Transaction: OP_Transaction, OP_Savepoint, OP_AutoCommit

**P4 union types:** String, Int64, Real, FuncDef, CollSeq, KeyInfo, Mem, SubProgram

**§10.8 Coroutines:**
VDBE uses coroutine-style execution for subqueries and multi-row returns:
- OP_InitCoroutine: Initialize coroutine state
- OP_Yield: Yield control between main program and coroutine
- OP_EndCoroutine: Clean up coroutine

Coroutines are NOT async — they are cooperative state machines within the VDBE VM.

### Unit Tests Required
1. test_all_opcode_dispatch: Every opcode enum variant has a handler
2. test_vdbe_op_encoding: VdbeOp struct serialization round-trip
3. test_control_flow_opcodes: Goto, If, IfNot, Halt, Return
4. test_comparison_opcodes: All 6 comparisons with type coercion
5. test_arithmetic_opcodes: Add/Sub/Mul/Div/Rem with overflow handling
6. test_cursor_seek_opcodes: SeekGE/GT/LE/LT with B-tree integration
7. test_coroutine_yield: Yield produces correct rows
8. test_sorter_opcodes: SorterOpen through SorterNext
9. test_aggregate_opcodes: AggStep + AggFinal for count/sum/avg

### E2E Test
Compile known SQL queries to VDBE bytecode. Execute and verify results match C sqlite3.
Log each opcode execution with register state for debugging.

","created_at":"2026-02-08T07:33:51Z"},{"id":1767,"issue_id":"bd-gird","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Missing Detail (Audit Fix): OP_NewRowid Concurrent-Mode Allocation Algorithm

### Problem
The bead documents VDBE opcodes and coroutines but does not detail how `OP_NewRowid` changes behavior under `BEGIN CONCURRENT`. This is a critical implementation detail since the standard `max(rowid)+1` approach fails with concurrent writers.

### Spec Content (§10.6 line 13537 + §5.10.1.1 lines 10043-10131)

**Normative rule:** In `BEGIN CONCURRENT`, `OP_NewRowid` MUST allocate via the snapshot-independent RowId allocator (§5.10.1.1), NOT by scanning the transaction's snapshot-visible `max(rowid)`. This is required for commutative insert merges and deterministic rebase to work for append-heavy workloads.

**Why standard approach fails:** C SQLite implements `OP_NewRowid` as `max(rowid)+1` because writers are serialized by the WAL write lock. In `BEGIN CONCURRENT`, two writers starting from the same snapshot would choose the same RowId, making `IntentOpKind::Insert { key: RowId, ... }` replay impossible (deterministic rebase would fail with `SQLITE_CONSTRAINT_PRIMARYKEY`).

**Minimum semantics (V1):**
- **Non-AUTOINCREMENT rowid tables:** Initialize the allocator (per schema epoch) to `max_committed_rowid(table) + 1` (computed by seeking to the rightmost committed row at the latest durable tip, NOT the transaction snapshot), then allocate monotonically. Allocations are NOT rolled back on abort; gaps are permitted.
- **AUTOINCREMENT tables:** Initialize to `max(sqlite_sequence.seq, max_committed_rowid(table)) + 1`. The committing transaction MUST persist AUTOINCREMENT state by updating `sqlite_sequence` to at least the maximum rowid actually inserted (monotone max merge: `seq = max(seq, inserted_rowid)`).
- **Bump-on-explicit-rowid:** If a statement inserts an explicit rowid `r`, the engine MUST ensure the allocator's next value is at least `r+1` (atomic max).
- **Cross-process:** Uses `ROWID_RESERVE` IPC wire message (kind_be=4) to reserve a monotone RowId range from the coordinator.
- **MAX_ROWID saturation:** The allocator MUST NOT allocate a RowId greater than `2^63-1`. If exceeded in `BEGIN CONCURRENT`, fail with `SQLITE_FULL`. Layer 1/Serialized mode retains C SQLite's random-rowid fallback.

**Allocated RowId stability:** The allocated RowId MUST be stable for the lifetime of the statement/transaction. Commit-time deterministic rebase (§5.10.2) MUST NOT change rowids, because that would invalidate `last_insert_rowid()` and RETURNING results.

## Test Requirements
- test_concurrent_newrowid_unique: Two concurrent transactions each insert 100 rows via auto-rowid; all 200 rowids are unique after both commit
- test_concurrent_newrowid_monotone: RowIds allocated in concurrent mode are monotonically increasing within each transaction
- test_concurrent_autoincrement_merge: Two concurrent transactions insert into AUTOINCREMENT table; sqlite_sequence reflects the max of both
- test_concurrent_explicit_rowid_bump: After explicit INSERT with rowid=1000, next auto-rowid >= 1001
- test_concurrent_newrowid_abort_gaps: Aborted transaction's rowids are NOT reused (gaps permitted)
- test_concurrent_max_rowid_full: Allocator near MAX_ROWID returns SQLITE_FULL in concurrent mode","created_at":"2026-02-08T07:45:58Z"},{"id":1768,"issue_id":"bd-gird","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## E2E Test (Named)

- test_e2e_bd_gird: implement the E2E plan already described in this bead; follow bd-1fpm for repro-bundle logging.","created_at":"2026-02-08T18:00:07Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-o0s0h","title":"WASM 4.5: Query result streaming via AsyncIterator for large result sets","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-02T04:22:08.775176207Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-02T04:22:56.810727535Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-o0s0h","depends_on_id":"bd-30o7g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-08T21:54:24Z","created_by":"import","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1978,"issue_id":"bd-o0s0h","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"# Task 4.5: Query Result Streaming

## What
For large result sets, provide AsyncIterator support to stream rows without loading everything into memory.

## API
```typescript
const stream = db.stream<User>('SELECT * FROM large_table');
for await (const row of stream) {
    process(row);
    if (done) break;  // Early termination supported
}
```

## Implementation
Uses Worker message protocol with chunked delivery. Worker sends rows in batches (e.g., 100 at a time) and pauses until the consumer is ready for more (backpressure).

## Acceptance Criteria
- AsyncIterator protocol compliance
- Backpressure support (Worker doesn't buffer unbounded)
- Early termination (break/return) properly cleans up
- Memory-efficient for million-row result sets

 {"id":"bd-o1ii4","title":"V1.5: PageData layout — Arc<[u8]> flat allocation replacing Arc<Vec<u8>>","description":"## What
`PageData` is currently `Arc<Vec<u8>>` (types/lib.rs:278) — double indirection: Arc pointer → Vec header → heap data. Replace with `Arc<[u8]>` which is a single contiguous allocation: Arc header + data bytes.

## Why  
Every page access follows two pointer chases: Arc→Vec→data. With `Arc<[u8]>`, it is one chase: Arc→data (contiguous). This saves one L1 cache miss per page access (~3-5ns). More importantly, `Arc<[u8]>` is smaller (16 bytes vs 24 bytes for Arc<Vec<u8>>) and does not carry Vec capacity metadata.

## Caveat — previous attempt failed
A prior attempt to change PageData to Arc<[u8]> was abandoned because `Arc::make_mut` requires `Sized` (Arc<[u8]> is unsized). The solution: for mutations, convert to `Vec<u8>` first, mutate, then re-wrap. Or use `Arc::get_mut()` which returns `Option<&mut [u8]>` for sole-owner cases.

## How
1. Change `PageData { data: Arc<Vec<u8>> }` to `PageData { data: Arc<[u8]> }`
2. `PageData::from_vec(v: Vec<u8>)` → `Arc::from(v)` (this allocates a contiguous block)
3. `as_bytes()` → `&*self.data` instead of `&**self.data`
4. `as_bytes_mut()` — cannot use Arc::make_mut on unsized. Use: check `Arc::get_mut()` (sole owner), else clone to Vec, mutate, re-wrap. This is rare (only B-tree page modifications).
5. Grep for all 574 PageData references and update

## Files
- `crates/fsqlite-types/src/lib.rs` — PageData struct
- ~574 references across all crates

## Estimated impact
3-5ns per page access × 250 pages per 10K INSERT = 750ns-1.25µs. Small.
But eliminates Vec metadata overhead and improves cache locality across ALL operations.

## Risk
HIGH — 574 references. Must be done carefully with extensive testing.
Consider: do this LAST, after V1.1-V1.4 and V2 are landed and verified.

## What
Comprehensive vitest test suite for the @frankensqlite/sdk package, testing all public API methods with detailed console logging.

## Test File Structure
```
packages/sdk/tests/
├── database.test.ts      # FrankenDB class tests
├── statement.test.ts     # PreparedStatement tests
├── transaction.test.ts   # Transaction tests
├── streaming.test.ts     # AsyncIterator tests
├── notifications.test.ts # Change notification tests
├── types.test.ts         # Value type conversion tests
├── errors.test.ts        # Error handling tests
├── concurrency.test.ts   # Concurrent query tests
└── setup.ts              # Shared test helpers
```

## Key Test Cases

### database.test.ts
```typescript
describe('FrankenDB', () => {
    let db: FrankenDB;
    
    beforeEach(async () => {
        console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] Setting up test database`);
        db = await FrankenDB.open(':memory:');
    });
    
    afterEach(async () => {
        await db.close();
        console.log(`[${new Date().toISOString()}] Test database closed`);
    });
    
    test('open and close lifecycle', async () => {
        console.log('  === open/close lifecycle ===');
        const db2 = await FrankenDB.open();
        expect(db2).toBeDefined();
        console.log('  Step 1: Database opened');
        await db2.close();
        console.log('  Step 2: Database closed');
        // Verify operations after close throw
        await expect(db2.execute('SELECT 1')).rejects.toThrow(/closed/);
        console.log('  Step 3: Post-close operation correctly rejected');
    });
    
    test('execute returns change count', async () => {
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE t(v INTEGER)');
        const changes = await db.execute('INSERT INTO t VALUES(1)');
        expect(changes).toBe(1);
        console.log(`  Changes: ${changes}`);
    });
    
    test('query returns typed results', async () => {
        interface Row { id: number; name: string; }
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE users(id INTEGER, name TEXT)');
        await db.execute("INSERT INTO users VALUES(1, 'Alice')");
        
        const rows = await db.query<Row>('SELECT * FROM users');
        expect(rows).toHaveLength(1);
        expect(rows[0].id).toBe(1);
        expect(rows[0].name).toBe('Alice');
        console.log(`  Query result: ${JSON.stringify(rows)}`);
    });
    
    test('queryOne returns single row or null', async () => {
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE t(v)');
        const empty = await db.queryOne('SELECT * FROM t');
        expect(empty).toBeNull();
        
        await db.execute("INSERT INTO t VALUES('x')");
        const row = await db.queryOne('SELECT * FROM t');
        expect(row).not.toBeNull();
        console.log(`  queryOne: null when empty, row when exists`);
    });
    
    test('parameterized queries prevent SQL injection', async () => {
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE t(name TEXT)');
        await db.execute("INSERT INTO t VALUES('safe')");
        
        // This should match nothing, not drop the table
        const malicious = "'; DROP TABLE t; --";
        const rows = await db.query('SELECT * FROM t WHERE name = ?', [malicious]);
        expect(rows).toHaveLength(0);
        
        // Table should still exist
        const count = await db.queryOne('SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM t');
        expect(count!.c).toBe(1);
        console.log('  SQL injection attempt safely handled');
    });
    
    test('handles 10000 rapid sequential queries', async () => {
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE bench(id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, v TEXT)');
        const start = Date.now();
        
        for (let i = 0; i < 10000; i++) {
            await db.execute('INSERT INTO bench VALUES(?, ?)', [i, `row_${i}`]);
        }
        
        const elapsed = Date.now() - start;
        console.log(`  10000 inserts: ${elapsed}ms (${(10000/elapsed*1000).toFixed(0)} ops/sec)`);
        
        const count = await db.queryOne<{c: number}>('SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM bench');
        expect(count!.c).toBe(10000);
    });
});
```

### transaction.test.ts
```typescript
describe('Transactions', () => {
    test('auto-commit on success', async () => {
        const db = await FrankenDB.open();
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE t(v INTEGER)');
        
        await db.transaction(async (tx) => {
            await tx.execute('INSERT INTO t VALUES(1)');
            await tx.execute('INSERT INTO t VALUES(2)');
        });
        
        const rows = await db.query('SELECT * FROM t');
        expect(rows).toHaveLength(2);
        console.log('  Auto-commit preserved 2 rows');
        await db.close();
    });
    
    test('auto-rollback on error', async () => {
        const db = await FrankenDB.open();
        await db.execute('CREATE TABLE t(v INTEGER)');
        await db.execute('INSERT INTO t VALUES(0)');
        
        await expect(db.transaction(async (tx) => {
            await tx.execute('INSERT INTO t VALUES(1)');
            throw new Error('intentional failure');
        })).rejects.toThrow('intentional failure');
        
        const rows = await db.query('SELECT * FROM t');
        expect(rows).toHaveLength(1); // Only the pre-existing row
        console.log('  Rollback removed transaction inserts');
        await db.close();
    });
    
    test('nested savepoints', async () => {
        // ...test savepoint behavior
    });
});
```

```bash
cd packages/sdk
npm test                     # vitest
npm test -- --reporter=verbose  # Detailed output
npm test -- --coverage       # With coverage report
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- 95%+ code coverage of SDK public API
- All tests have console.log timestamps
- Performance benchmarks embedded (ops/sec for common operations)
- Error paths tested (closed db, invalid SQL, type mismatches)
- Concurrent query handling verified
- Memory leak checks (open/close cycles)

 {"id":"bd-ooc2c","title":"Track M: Epoch-Based Reclamation — Replace Arc<> with Zero-Cost Epoch Pinning","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:15:46.513200471Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-10T22:50:23.959763185Z","closed_at":"2026-04-10T22:50:23.959358538Z","close_reason":"Safe thread-local value slab fallback verified and landed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ooc2c","depends_on_id":"bd-wi4w4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-31T19:51:17.697600286Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":3384,"issue_id":"bd-ooc2c","author":"ubuntu","text":"## Problem (from alien-graveyard §14.10)
Every SqliteValue::Text and SqliteValue::Blob uses Arc<> for reference counting. Arc::clone() costs ~5 ns (atomic fetch_add with SeqCst). Arc::drop() costs ~5 ns (atomic fetch_sub + possible dealloc). For a 3-column row, that's 6 atomic operations = ~30 ns of pure reference counting overhead.

## Solution: crossbeam-epoch Pinning
Replace Arc<str> with epoch-pinned raw pointers. Readers pin the current epoch (1 atomic store, amortized across many reads). Values are retired to epoch garbage lists. When all readers advance past the epoch, values are freed in bulk. Per-access cost: ~1 ns (epoch check) vs ~10 ns (Arc clone+drop).

## Why This Is Safe
crossbeam-epoch is already a dependency (used for MVCC). The epoch pinning API is well-tested. Values allocated in an epoch arena are guaranteed to live until all readers advance. This is the same pattern used by LeanStore, Silo, and Hekaton.

## Expected Impact: -0.03 to -0.07 µs per row (eliminates all atomic ref counting in hot path)

## Fallback
If epoch pinning proves too complex for SqliteValue, keep Arc but use a thread-local cache of recently freed values (slab allocator) to amortize alloc/dealloc costs. This is simpler and still saves ~0.02 µs.
